messages.content: What is the Wade-Giles spelling of the name "He Xuefeng"?
The Wade-Giles spelling of the name "He Xuefeng" would be "Ho Hseuh-feng".

"Gong Zhi
Shao Tu
He Xuefeng
Chen Fang
Meng Mu
Jiang Qing
Cao Ling
Zi Jian
Dai Hanying
Wei Lingxin"

According to Wade-Giles spelling system, the names would be written as follows:

Gong Chih
Shao T'u
Ho Hsüeh-feng
Ch'en Fang
Meng Mu
Chiang Ch'ing
Ts'ao Ling
Tzu Chien
Tai Han-ying
Wei Ling-hsin
"Tang Dong"
The Wade-Giles spelling of the name "Tang Dong" would be "T'ang Tung".
"Deng Chang"
The Wade-Giles spelling of the name "Deng Chang" would be "Teng Chang".
"Cao Xiang"
The Wade-Giles spelling of the name "Cao Xiang" would be "Ts'ao Hsiang".
"Huang Guanting"
The Wade-Giles spelling of the name "Huang Guanting" would be "Huang Kuan-ting".
Who was the President of Taiwan in 2000?
The president of Taiwan in 2000 was Chen Shui-bian. He served as the president from 2000 to 2008.
What is the Pinyin spelling of "Chen Shui-bian"?
The Pinyin spelling of "Chen Shui-bian" is "Chén Shuǐbiān".
How did Chen Shui-bian become President?

Chen Shui-bian became president of Taiwan through the 2000 presidential election. He was the candidate for the Democratic Progressive Party (DPP). Chen Shui-bian won the election, defeating his opponents and becoming the country's first non-Kuomintang (KMT) president. His victory marked a significant turning point in Taiwanese politics, as it ended the KMT's long-standing dominance in the presidency.
What role did the National Assembly play in the election of Chen Shui-bian?

The National Assembly played a significant role in the election of Chen Shui-bian as president of Taiwan in 2000. At that time, the president and vice president were elected by the National Assembly, which was a body comprised of delegates representing various political parties and interest groups.

In the 2000 election, the Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) candidate, Chen Shui-bian, was running against the Kuomintang (KMT) candidate, Lien Chan. The National Assembly, which was dominated by the KMT, held the power to choose the president.

However, due to mounting pressure for democratic reforms and calls for a more direct method of presidential elections, a constitutional amendment was passed in 1997. This amendment allowed for the first direct presidential election to take place in 2000.

Although the National Assembly still had the authority to select the president, the election signaled a shift toward direct democracy. Chen Shui-bian won the popular vote in the presidential election, which led to his historic victory as the first non-KMT president of Taiwan. The direct election system was fully implemented in subsequent elections, abolishing the role of the National Assembly in electing the president.
Did the National Assembly for anything besides electing a President, amending the constitutions, and during times of crisis?

Yes, the National Assembly in Taiwan had additional functions beyond electing the president, amending the constitution, and addressing times of crisis. 

One important role of the National Assembly was to ratify treaties and agreements with foreign countries. Prior to the Constitutional reforms in 2005, the National Assembly approval was required for treaties to become legally binding.

Furthermore, the National Assembly served as a platform for deliberations on national policies and legislation. It functioned as a forum where various political parties and interest groups could discuss and debate important issues. Discussions related to legislation, policies, and national development were conducted within the National Assembly.

Additionally, the National Assembly had the authority to impeach and remove the president and vice president from office in case of serious misconduct or constitutional violations. This impeachment process involved investigation, hearings, and voting by the National Assembly members.

However, it's worth noting that the powers of the National Assembly were significantly curtailed by constitutional reforms in 1997 and 2005. The body lost much of its authority over time, and gradually its role became more symbolic. In 2005, the National Assembly was officially abolished, and the constitutional amendments were shifted to the legislature for approval.
